summary: Hillel Wayne explains why he prefers Windows over other operating systems despite its shortcomings: AutoHotKey makes customization trivially easy. He walks through progressively complex scripts he's written, from simple mouse button remapping to automated sci-hub paper retrieval. The key insight is that while similar functionality might be achievable on Mac or Linux, the friction of implementation matters enormously. When adding affordances is easy, you actually add them; when it requires diving into OS internals, you don't bother.
key_points: AutoHotKey hooks into the entire Windows system, making it easy to augment workflows without understanding OS internals
Small affordances compound: the difference between 'a single hotkey' and 'four steps' dramatically affects whether you'll actually use something
Muscle memory matters for productivity - alt+tab fails because the window stack is unpredictable, but fixed hotkeys work reliably
The tool's accessibility trumps its elegance - AHK's language is admittedly ugly but the low barrier to entry makes customization actually happen
Portability of scripts beats registry hacks or system-level configurations that don't transfer to new machines
money_quotes: There's something about the difference between 'a single hotkey' and 'four steps' that makes me more likely to bother.
When adding affordances to my computer is easy, I'm more likely to add them.
Most my AutoHotKey uses are like this: tacking on a crude API on to something that doesn't have one.
As a hard-core Vim user, anything that gets in the way of muscle memory is bad and should be avoided.
I wouldn't call it particularly elegant, and it's filled with tons of redundancy and quirks. Even its fans admit how nasty the language can be.
Using AutoHotKey makes me feel like I can customize my machine. My scripts bring the computer closer to what I need.
key_insight: The friction of customization matters more than the possibility of customization - when adding small workflow improvements is easy, you actually do it; when it requires deep technical knowledge, you don't.
